message:
Close phage_explorer-bjoz.7: visualization primitives complete

All reusable visualization primitives implemented:
- HeatmapCanvas: 2D heatmap with color scales
- ScatterCanvas: High-performance scatter plots
- ArcDiagram: Arc/chord diagrams for connections
- GenomeTrack: Linear genome visualization
- GelCanvas: Virtual gel electrophoresis
- ColorLegend: Color scale legends
- colorScales: Color interpolation utilities

This unblocks 10 dependent overlay beads.

🤖 Generated with Claude Code

 {"id":"phage_explorer-9jc","title":"Advanced: Cocktail Resistance Evolution Simulator","description":"Simulate resistance emergence under single vs cocktail regimens using Gillespie/tau-leaping. Params from genome: receptor diversity, Sie genes, CRISPR spacer matches. Live ASCII trajectories, risk meter, side-by-side mono vs cocktail. Turns genomic evidence into dosing/risk guidance. Novelty: High, Pedagogical: High (stochastic processes, evolutionary dynamics).","notes":"## COCKTAIL RESISTANCE EVOLUTION SIMULATOR

**Full Implementation from Roadmap**

### Concept
Simulate resistance emergence under single vs cocktail regimens using genome-derived parameters (receptor diversity, anti-defense, spacer proximity).

### How to Build

**Stochastic Engine:**
- Gillespie/tau-leaping in Rust+WASM (rand + rayon)
- Events: bacterial growth, phage adsorption, resistance mutation, receptor switching

**Genome-Derived Parameters:**
- Receptor diversity from tail fiber annotation
- Sie genes (superinfection exclusion)
- CRISPR spacer matches (immunity potential)
- Anti-defense gene count

**User Inputs:**
- MOI (multiplicity of infection)
- Dosing cadence (hours between doses)
- Population size (CFU/mL)
- Cocktail composition (1-5 phages)

**Outputs:**
- Probability of resistance over time
- Time-to-resistance distribution
- Compare mono vs cocktail curves
- Optimal cocktail selection

### TUI Features
- Live ASCII trajectories (multiple runs overlaid)
- Risk meter updates in real-time
- Side-by-side mono vs cocktail comparison
- Sliders: MOI, dose interval, population size
- Toggle 'optimize cocktail' to auto-pick best trio
- Resistance probability timeline
- [R]un new simulation, [C]ompare, [O]ptimize

### Why It's Good
- Turns genomic evidence into therapy guidance
- Quantifies resistance risk over time
- Compares mono vs cocktail robustness
- Identifies synergistic combinations

### Implementation Stack
- Rust+WASM for Gillespie simulator (rand, rayon)
- TS/Ink UI for sliders and live trajectories
- SQLite cache for genome-derived parameters
- ASCII braille for trajectory plots

Novelty: 9/10, Pedagogical: 9/10, Wow Factor: 9/10","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-06T23:55:45.232996-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T18:20:45.871789-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-9jc","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-ojb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-06T23:56:51.540252-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

-{"id":"phage_explorer-9wby","title":"Dual-row DNA+AA inline translation view","description":"# Task: Dual-row DNA+AA inline translation view

## Problem
Currently users must toggle between DNA and AA view modes. For codon-level analysis, seeing both simultaneously is essential—DNA sequence with its translation aligned below.

## Solution
Implement a dual-row rendering mode in CanvasSequenceGridRenderer that shows:

```
Position: 1    4    7    10   13   16   19...
DNA:      ATG  GAC  TTA  GCC  AAA  TGT  CAG...
AA:        M    D    L    A    K    C    Q ...
```

## Technical Design

### Layout Structure
- Row 1 (top): DNA triplets with codon grouping visual cues
- Row 2 (bottom): Single AA centered under each codon
- Visual separator line between rows
- Codon boundary markers (subtle vertical lines every 3bp)

### State Management
```typescript
// Add to store
viewMode: dna | aa | dual  // Add dual option

// Add to SequenceGridOptions
dualRowMode?: boolean;
codonGrouping?: boolean;  // Show visual codon boundaries
```

### Rendering Logic
```typescript
// In CanvasSequenceGridRenderer
private renderDualRow(ctx, range, layout) {
  const codonStart = Math.floor(range.startIndex / 3) * 3;
  
  for (let i = codonStart; i < range.endIndex; i += 3) {
    // Render DNA row (3 nucleotides)
    this.renderCodon(ctx, i, rowY: 0);
    
    // Render AA row (1 amino acid centered)
    const aa = translateCodon(sequence.slice(i, i+3));
    this.renderAA(ctx, aa, i, rowY: 1);
  }
}
```

## Files to Modify

- `/packages/web/src/rendering/CanvasSequenceGridRenderer.ts` - Add dual-row render path
- `/packages/state/src/store.ts` - Add dual view mode
- `/packages/web/src/components/SequenceView.tsx` - Add UI toggle for dual mode
- `/packages/web/src/hooks/useSequenceGrid.ts` - Support dual mode

## Cell Sizing for Dual Mode

| Zoom Level | DNA Cell | AA Cell | Total Row Height |
|------------|----------|---------|------------------|
| Codon (1.0x) | 8×10px | 24×12px | 24px |
| Base (2.0x) | 16×16px | 48×18px | 36px |
| Ultra (4.0x) | 32×24px | 96×28px | 56px |

## Visual Design

- DNA row: Standard nucleotide colors
- AA row: Property-based coloring (hydrophobic=yellow, polar=green, charged=blue/red)
- Codon boundaries: Subtle 1px line every 3bp
- Reading frame indicator: Highlight current frame offset

## Dependencies

- Core translateCodon function (exists in @phage-explorer/core)
- GlyphAtlas (for AA rendering)
- Store update for new view mode

 {"id":"phage_explorer-9wno","title":"Fix canvas sequence grid scaling and reduced-motion update","description":"CanvasSequenceGridRenderer scales 2D context on every resize (no reset), causing exponential transform and blurry rendering. reducedMotion/postProcess changes are ignored because renderer is not updated after init. Fix: reset transforms on resize; add setter methods; update hook to propagate reducedMotion/postProcess and rebuild on scanlines/glow/postProcess changes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-08T00:23:50.262024-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-08T00:41:27.752157-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-08T00:41:27.752157-05:00"}

 {"id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.4","title":"CRISPROverlay - CRISPR spacer mapping and visualization","description":"Implement overlay for mapping CRISPR spacers to phage genomes and visualizing host immune targeting patterns.

## Biological Significance
CRISPR-Cas systems provide bacteria with adaptive immunity against phages. This overlay:
- Maps known CRISPR spacers to phage genome positions
- Reveals targeted regions (often essential genes)
- Shows evolutionary arms race signatures
- Identifies anti-CRISPR (Acr) protein candidates
- Predicts host range based on CRISPR resistance

Understanding CRISPR targeting helps predict:
- Which hosts this phage can infect
- Selective pressure on phage genes
- Evolution of escape mutations

## Visualization Type
Multi-component display:
- **Genome track**: Linear genome with spacer match positions
- **Arc diagram**: Connections from spacers to genome targets
- **Heatmap**: Spacer match density across genome
- **Target gene list**: Sorted by number of spacer matches
- **Host resistance panel**: Predicted CRISPR-sensitive hosts

## Worker Integration
Create `workers/crispr-worker.ts`:
- Search for CRISPR spacer matches (exact and fuzzy)
- Allow up to 2 mismatches (escape mutations)
- Identify protospacer adjacent motifs (PAMs) near matches
- Calculate spacer coverage across genome
- Detect anti-CRISPR protein candidates (Acr HMMs)
- Integrate with CRISPRCasdb or other spacer databases
- Predict host susceptibility based on spacer matches

## Implementation Details

### Files to Create
1. `src/tui/overlays/CRISPROverlay.tsx`
   - Genome track with spacer match markers (GenomeTrack)
   - Arc diagram linking spacers to targets (ArcDiagram)
   - Heatmap of spacer density (HeatmapCanvas)
   - Targeted gene table
   - Host resistance predictions panel

2. `src/workers/crispr-worker.ts`
   - Spacer sequence search (Boyer-Moore or FM-index)
   - Fuzzy matching with mismatches
   - PAM identification (regex patterns for different Cas types)
   - Acr protein detection (HMM scanning or profile matching)
   - Database integration (fetch spacers from CRISPRCasdb API)
   - Host range prediction logic

3. `src/data/spacer-db.ts` (optional)
   - Local cache of common CRISPR spacers
   - Fallback if external API unavailable

### Dependencies
- **Requires**: Visualization primitives task (GenomeTrack, ArcDiagram, HeatmapCanvas)
- Set dependency after primitives task is created

### Keyboard Shortcut
- **Alt+C** - Toggle CRISPR overlay

### Data Flow
1. User activates overlay (Alt+C)
2. If online: Worker queries CRISPRCasdb API with phage genus/species
3. If offline: Worker uses local spacer database (if available)
4. Worker receives spacer sequences and host information
5. Worker searches phage genome:
   - Exact matches (perfect complementarity)
   - 1-mismatch tolerance (common escape mutations)
   - 2-mismatch tolerance (rare escape variants)
6. Worker identifies PAMs adjacent to matches:
   - 5'-NGG-3' for SpyCas9
   - 5'-YTN-3' for SauCas9
   - Other PAMs for different Cas types
7. Worker calculates:
   - Spacer match density per 1kb window
   - Target gene vulnerability scores
   - Anti-CRISPR protein predictions
8. UI renders all visualization components
9. User can:
   - Hover spacer arc to see host source
   - Click target gene for details
   - Toggle mismatch tolerance (0/1/2)
   - Export spacer match table

## Display Components

### 1. Genome Track with Spacer Matches
- Vertical markers at each match position
- Color by mismatch count: Blue (0), Green (1), Yellow (2)
- Marker height = match quality score

### 2. Arc Diagram
- Arcs connect spacer source (top) to genome target (bottom)
- Color by host organism
- Thickness by match quality
- Grouped by host species

### 3. Spacer Density Heatmap
- Sliding window (1kb) spacer match density
- Reveals hotspots (highly targeted regions)
- Color scale: White (0) → Red (high density)

### 4. Targeted Gene Table
| Gene | Position | Spacers | Hosts | Function |
|------|----------|---------|-------|----------|
| MCP  | 12345    | 15      | 8     | Major capsid |
| TerL | 23456    | 12      | 6     | Terminase    |

Sorted by spacer count.

### 5. Host Resistance Panel
- List of hosts with spacers targeting this phage
- Predicted infection outcome:
  - ✓ Resistant (many spacers, essential genes targeted)
  - ~ Partial (few spacers, non-essential targets)
  - ✗ Susceptible (no spacers or Acr proteins present)

### 6. Anti-CRISPR Detection
- If Acr proteins predicted, highlight genes
- Link to Acr family annotations

## Spacer Match Scoring
For each match, calculate quality score:
```
score = 100 - (mismatches × 25) + (PAM_present × 20)
```
- Perfect match + PAM: 120
- 1 mismatch + PAM: 95
- 2 mismatches + PAM: 70
- Perfect match, no PAM: 100

## Biological Interpretation

### Highly Targeted Genes
Essential genes (DNA polymerase, terminase, major capsid) often have many spacers, indicating strong selective pressure.

### Spacer-Free Regions
Regions with no spacer matches may:
- Contain non-essential genes
- Have high mutation rates (escape)
- Be recently acquired (HGT)

### Acr Proteins
Presence of anti-CRISPR proteins suggests:
- Phage can evade CRISPR immunity
- Arms race with specific host lineages
- Broader host range potential

## Testing Considerations
- Validate with well-characterized phage-CRISPR interactions (E. coli K12 vs phages)
- Check PAM detection accuracy
- Compare Acr predictions with experimental data
- Test performance with large spacer databases (>10K spacers)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:00.311741-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:00.311741-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.4","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:00.31252-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.4","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:09:49.697639-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.5","title":"SyntenyOverlay - Gene order conservation blocks","description":"Implement overlay for detecting and visualizing synteny (conserved gene order) blocks between the current phage and related genomes.

## Biological Significance
Synteny analysis reveals:
- Conserved functional modules (gene cassettes)
- Genome rearrangements and recombination events
- Evolutionary relationships between phages
- Mosaic genome architecture
- Essential vs flexible genomic regions

Phages often share syntenic blocks (e.g., structural module, replication module) even when overall similarity is low, reflecting modular evolution.

## Visualization Type
Interactive comparison view:
- **Main panel**: Dot plot matrix (self vs reference genomes)
- **Alignment tracks**: Linear synteny blocks with ribbons connecting homologs
- **Rearrangement diagram**: Inversions, translocations, duplications
- **Conservation heatmap**: Per-gene synteny conservation score

## Worker Integration
Create `workers/synteny-worker.ts`:
- Perform all-vs-all gene similarity searches (DIAMOND-like local alignment)
- Identify collinear gene clusters (syntenic blocks)
- Detect genome rearrangements (inversions, transpositions)
- Calculate synteny conservation scores
- Cluster phages by synteny patterns
- Generate dot plot matrices

## Implementation Details

### Files to Create
1. `src/tui/overlays/SyntenyOverlay.tsx`
   - Dot plot visualization (HeatmapCanvas for matrix)
   - Linear synteny track display (GenomeTrack + ribbons)
   - Rearrangement diagram
   - Reference genome selector
   - Conservation score display

2. `src/workers/synteny-worker.ts`
   - Gene-to-gene similarity scoring (Smith-Waterman or BLAST-like)
   - Syntenic block detection (MCScanX-like algorithm)
   - Collinearity scoring
   - Rearrangement classification
   - Dot plot generation
   - Multi-genome comparison support

3. `src/data/reference-genomes.ts` (optional)
   - Curated set of reference phage genomes
   - Common phage families (T4-like, T7-like, lambda-like, etc.)

### Dependencies
- **Requires**: Visualization primitives task (HeatmapCanvas, GenomeTrack)
- Set dependency after primitives task is created

### Keyboard Shortcut
- **Alt+S** - Toggle synteny overlay

### Data Flow
1. User activates overlay (Alt+S)
2. UI prompts for reference genome selection:
   - Upload custom genome file
   - Select from built-in references
   - Query NCBI for related phages
3. Worker receives both genomes (query + reference)
4. Worker performs all-vs-all gene comparison:
   - Extract protein sequences
   - Local alignment (Smith-Waterman)
   - E-value filtering (< 1e-5)
   - % identity and coverage thresholds
5. Worker detects syntenic blocks:
   - Find chains of collinear homologs
   - Require ≥3 consecutive genes
   - Allow gaps (up to 2 intervening genes)
6. Worker classifies rearrangements:
   - Inversions (reverse strand order)
   - Translocations (position changes)
   - Duplications (copy number > 1)
7. Worker generates visualizations:
   - Dot plot matrix
   - Synteny ribbons
   - Conservation scores
8. UI renders comparison view
9. User can:
   - Pan/zoom dot plot
   - Click synteny block for gene details
   - Toggle multiple reference genomes
   - Export synteny block coordinates

## Visualization Panels

### 1. Dot Plot Matrix
- X-axis: Query genome position
- Y-axis: Reference genome position
- Dots: Gene homologs
- Diagonal lines: Syntenic blocks
- Off-diagonal: Rearrangements
- Color by % identity

### 2. Linear Synteny Tracks
```
Query:    [gene1] [gene2] [gene3] ─────── [gene4] [gene5]
             │       │       │                │       │
             └───────┴───────┘                └───────┘
Reference:[gene1] [gene2] [gene3] [geneX] [gene4] [gene5]
```
Ribbons connect homologous genes. Width = % identity.

### 3. Rearrangement Diagram
Visual representation of:
- **Inversions**: Curved arcs with direction arrows
- **Translocations**: Crossed ribbons
- **Duplications**: Branching ribbons
- **Deletions**: Gaps in reference

### 4. Conservation Heatmap
Per-gene bar chart showing:
- Synteny conservation score (0-100)
- Number of syntenic references
- Color gradient: Red (unique) → Green (highly conserved)

## Synteny Block Scoring
For each block, calculate conservation score:
```
score = (num_collinear_genes / block_length) × 
        (avg_pairwise_identity / 100) × 
        (1 - gap_penalty)
```
- Perfect collinearity, 100% ID, no gaps: 100
- Partial conservation: 50-90
- Weak synteny: 20-50

## Biological Interpretation

### Strong Synteny
- Conserved functional modules (replication, packaging)
- Essential gene order (operons, regulatory coupling)
- Recent common ancestry

### Rearrangements
- Inversions: Often neutral, genome plasticity
- Translocations: Recombination, horizontal exchange
- Duplications: Gene family expansion, functional redundancy

### Mosaic Structure
Phages with multiple syntenic blocks from different sources indicate:
- Modular evolution
- Recombination hotspots
- Hybrid genome architecture

## Reference Genome Sources
Built-in references (examples):
- **T4-like**: Myoviridae, large genome, complex structure
- **T7-like**: Podoviridae, small genome, simple
- **Lambda-like**: Siphoviridae, temperate, integration
- **User-uploaded**: Custom comparison

## Performance Optimization
- Pre-compute common reference comparisons
- Cache gene similarity matrices
- Incremental dot plot rendering for large genomes
- Web Worker offloading for alignment calculations

## Testing Considerations
- Validate with known syntenic phage pairs (T4 vs T4-like)
- Test rearrangement detection (T7 vs T3)
- Compare with established tools (MCScanX, SyMAP)
- Check performance with divergent genomes","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:02.028256-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:02.028256-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.5","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:02.029077-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.5","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:09:50.563742-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.6","title":"TropismOverlay - Host receptor predictions","description":"Implement overlay for predicting host tropism (host range) based on receptor-binding protein analysis and host factor requirements.

## Biological Significance
Host tropism determines:
- Which bacterial species/strains a phage can infect
- Receptor specificity (LPS, pili, flagella, proteins)
- Therapeutic applications (target specificity for phage therapy)
- Ecological niche and biogeography
- Evolutionary constraints on host switching

This overlay analyzes receptor-binding proteins (RBPs) and auxiliary host recognition factors to predict host range.

## Visualization Type
Multi-panel dashboard:
- **RBP structure diagram**: Predicted receptor-binding protein domains
- **Host prediction table**: Ranked list of potential hosts with confidence
- **Receptor type radar chart**: Predicted specificity (LPS, pili, OmpC, etc.)
- **Host phylogeny tree**: Mapped predicted host range
- **Confidence heatmap**: Per-host-species infection probability

## Worker Integration
Create `workers/tropism-worker.ts`:
- Identify receptor-binding proteins (tail fibers, tail spikes, baseplate proteins)
- Predict protein domains using HMMs (Pfam, custom phage RBP profiles)
- Match domains to known receptor specificities
- Analyze auxiliary host factors (DNA modifications, restriction systems)
- Query host-phage interaction databases (PHASTER, Millard database)
- Calculate host range predictions with confidence scores
- Consider host defense systems (R-M, CRISPR)

## Implementation Details

### Files to Create
1. `src/tui/overlays/TropismOverlay.tsx`
   - Multi-panel dashboard layout
   - RBP domain visualization (modular protein diagram)
   - Host prediction table with sorting
   - Receptor radar chart
   - Phylogenetic tree with host mapping
   - Confidence heatmap

2. `src/workers/tropism-worker.ts`
   - RBP identification (regex + HMMs)
   - Domain prediction and classification
   - Receptor specificity database matching
   - Host range prediction algorithm
   - Defense system compatibility check
   - Phylogenetic mapping

3. `src/data/rbp-profiles.ts`
   - HMM profiles for common RBP domains
   - Receptor specificity annotations
   - Known phage-host interaction database

4. `src/data/host-phylogeny.ts` (optional)
   - Bacterial phylogenetic tree structure
   - Host taxonomy database

### Keyboard Shortcut
- **Alt+T** - Toggle tropism overlay

### Data Flow
1. User activates overlay (Alt+T)
2. Worker receives genome sequence and ORF annotations
3. Worker identifies RBP candidates:
   - Search for tail fiber proteins (T4-like, T7-like)
   - Identify tail spike proteins (P22-like)
   - Find baseplate proteins (Myoviridae)
   - Detect pili/flagella binding proteins
4. Worker predicts domains:
   - Scan RBP sequences with HMM profiles
   - Classify domains (pectin lyase, endosialidase, carbohydrate-binding, etc.)
   - Map to receptor specificities
5. Worker queries databases:
   - Match RBP signatures to known phage-host interactions
   - Check compatibility with host defense systems
6. Worker calculates host predictions:
   - Rank hosts by likelihood (0-100 score)
   - Aggregate evidence from multiple sources
7. UI renders dashboard with predictions
8. User can:
   - View RBP domain details
   - Sort hosts by confidence
   - Export host range predictions
   - Compare with experimental data (if available)

## Prediction Algorithm
Host range score calculated from:
- **RBP domain match (40%)**: Direct domain-to-receptor mapping
- **Homolog database (30%)**: Similarity to phages with known hosts
- **Auxiliary factors (20%)**: DNA modification compatibility, lack of CRISPR spacers
- **Phylogenetic proximity (10%)**: Evolutionary distance from known hosts

Final score: 0-100 (>70 = high confidence, 50-70 = medium, <50 = low)

## Display Panels

### 1. RBP Structure Diagram
Modular visualization of receptor-binding protein:
```
[Signal] [Shaft] [Neck] [Head] [Binding Domain]
  (aa 1-50) (50-300) (300-400) (400-600) (600-800)
                                        │
                                  [Pectin Lyase]
                                  → LPS receptor
```
Domains color-coded by function.

### 2. Host Prediction Table
| Host Species | Confidence | Receptor | Evidence |
|--------------|------------|----------|----------|
| E. coli K12  | 95         | LPS O-antigen | RBP domain + DB match |
| Salmonella   | 78         | LPS core | RBP homology |
| Shigella     | 65         | OmpA | Weak domain match |

Sortable by confidence.

### 3. Receptor Type Radar Chart
Polar chart showing predicted specificity for:
- LPS (lipopolysaccharide)
- Pili (F-pili, Type IV pili)
- Flagella
- Outer membrane proteins (OmpC, OmpF, etc.)
- Capsule polysaccharides
- Other receptors

### 4. Host Phylogeny Tree
Phylogenetic tree of bacteria with:
- Predicted hosts highlighted (color by confidence)
- Known hosts marked (if data available)
- Evolutionary context for host range

### 5. Confidence Heatmap
Matrix showing infection probability:
- Rows: Bacterial species
- Columns: Evidence types (RBP, DB, Defense)
- Color intensity: Confidence (0-100)

## RBP Domain Types
Common domains and their receptor targets:
- **Pectin lyase**: LPS O-antigen
- **Endosialidase**: Sialic acid (capsule)
- **Carbohydrate-binding**: Polysaccharide receptors
- **Pili-binding**: F-pili, Type IV pili
- **OmpC/F-binding**: Outer membrane proteins
- **Baseplate**: Complex multi-receptor binding

## Evidence Sources
1. **Direct domain match**: RBP domain has known receptor
2. **Homolog database**: Similar phages infect these hosts
3. **Defense compatibility**: No CRISPR spacers, compatible R-M
4. **Phylogenetic inference**: Close relatives of known hosts
5. **Experimental data**: Published host range (if available)

## Biological Interpretation

### Narrow Host Range
- Highly specific RBP domains
- Stringent auxiliary factor requirements
- Example: Lambda (E. coli K12 only)

### Broad Host Range
- Generic receptors (common OMPs)
- Flexible auxiliary factor tolerance
- Multiple RBPs or modular binding
- Example: T4 (many E. coli strains)

### Host Switching Potential
- RBP domain plasticity
- Low defense system barriers
- Mosaic RBP structure (recombination)

## Testing Considerations
- Validate with phages of known host range (T4, T7, Lambda)
- Compare predictions with experimental plating assays
- Check sensitivity/specificity of RBP domain detection
- Test with polyvalent phages (multiple hosts)","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:04.209758-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-08T15:55:08.748666-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.6","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:04.211733-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
-{"id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.7","title":"Create reusable visualization primitives","description":"Create a set of reusable, high-performance canvas-based visualization primitives that will be shared across multiple overlay components in Phase 3.

## Purpose
Provide consistent, performant rendering components for complex genomic visualizations including heatmaps, scatter plots, arc diagrams, genome tracks, and gel simulations.

## Components to Create

### 1. HeatmapCanvas.tsx
- Generic 2D heatmap renderer with configurable color scales
- Support for rectangular and triangular matrices
- Mouse interaction (hover, tooltips, selection)
- Color interpolation and legend integration
- Used by: DotPlotOverlay, SyntenyOverlay, StructureConstraintOverlay

### 2. ScatterCanvas.tsx
- High-performance scatter plot renderer for large point clouds
- PCA/dimensionality reduction visualization support
- Point clustering and selection
- Axis labels, gridlines, and legends
- Used by: PhasePortraitOverlay, BiasDecompositionOverlay

### 3. ArcDiagram.tsx
- Arc/chord diagram for relational data
- Bezier curve rendering for connections
- Bundling and collision detection
- Used by: HGTOverlay, CRISPROverlay, structural feature connections

### 4. GenomeTrack.tsx
- Linear genome track visualization component
- Support for multiple track types (bars, lines, regions)
- Scrolling/panning with position synchronization
- Used by: Multiple overlays showing positional data

### 5. GelCanvas.tsx
- Virtual gel electrophoresis simulation renderer
- Band rendering with intensity gradients
- Size marker integration
- Used by: GelOverlay

## Technical Requirements
- TypeScript with strict typing
- Canvas-based rendering for performance
- WebGL fallback for very large datasets
- Consistent event handling interface
- Theme-aware color schemes
- Accessibility considerations (ARIA labels)

## File Structure
```
src/tui/overlays/primitives/
  ├── HeatmapCanvas.tsx
  ├── ScatterCanvas.tsx
  ├── ArcDiagram.tsx
  ├── GenomeTrack.tsx
  ├── GelCanvas.tsx
  └── types.ts (shared types and interfaces)
```

## Performance Targets
- 60 FPS rendering for interactive updates
- Support for 10K+ data points in scatter plots
- Efficient canvas updates using dirty regions
- RequestAnimationFrame for smooth animations

## Dependencies
- Depends on Phase 2 worker infrastructure completion
- Required by most Phase 3 overlay components","notes":"PinkMountain working on visualization primitives; auditing primitives dirs and aligning overlays","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:57.555398-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-08T23:05:24.161106-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.7","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:57.557031-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.7","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-qiaq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:57.559939-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.8","title":"PackagingPressureOverlay - DNA packaging constraints","description":"Implement overlay for visualizing DNA packaging pressure and constraints in phage capsids.

## Biological Significance
DNA packaging in phages is one of the most physically demanding processes in biology. The viral genome is compressed to near-crystalline densities, creating internal pressures of tens of atmospheres. This overlay reveals:
- Regions under high torsional stress during packaging
- AT-rich vs GC-rich content effects on packaging efficiency
- Predicted packaging motor pause sites
- Capsid filling progression and pressure buildup

## Visualization Type
Multi-track canvas visualization with:
- Linear pressure gradient track (0-100% genome position)
- Local GC% correlation to packaging resistance
- Predicted portal motor pause sites (vertical markers)
- Cumulative pressure curve
- Color coding: Green (low pressure) → Yellow → Red (high pressure)

## Worker Integration
Create `workers/packaging-worker.ts`:
- Calculate local DNA bend angles and flexibility
- Compute cumulative torsional stress
- Predict packaging motor pause sites based on sequence features
- Estimate internal capsid pressure at different filling stages
- Uses thermodynamic models (e.g., Kindt et al. 2001)

## Implementation Details

### Files to Create
1. `src/tui/overlays/PackagingPressureOverlay.tsx`
   - Main overlay component with canvas rendering
   - Pressure track visualization
   - Interactive position selection
   - Legend and scale indicators

2. `src/workers/packaging-worker.ts`
   - DNA mechanics calculations
   - Torsional stress modeling
   - Pause site prediction algorithms

### Keyboard Shortcut
- **Alt+P** - Toggle packaging pressure overlay

### Data Flow
1. User activates overlay (Alt+P)
2. Worker receives genome sequence
3. Worker calculates:
   - Sliding window GC% and AT% composition
   - Local DNA bend angles (using dinucleotide parameters)
   - Cumulative packaging resistance
   - Motor pause probability at each position
4. UI renders multi-track visualization
5. User can hover for position-specific pressure values

## Scientific References
- Kindt et al. (2001) DNA packaging and ejection forces
- Smith et al. (2001) Single-molecule packaging dynamics
- Evilevitch et al. (2003) Internal capsid pressure measurements

## Testing Considerations
- Validate with known high-pressure phages (T4, lambda)
- Check correlation with experimental packaging rates
- Verify pause site predictions match biochemical data","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:59.033222-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-08T17:08:25.226462-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.8","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:59.033939-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.8","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-qiaq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:00:59.035471-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.9","title":"VirionStabilityOverlay - Virion structural stability predictions","description":"Implement overlay for predicting and visualizing virion structural stability based on capsid protein properties and genome features.

## Biological Significance
Virion stability determines:
- Environmental persistence (temperature, pH, desiccation resistance)
- Host range constraints (survival in mucus, digestive systems)
- Therapeutic applications (phage therapy viability)
- Evolutionary fitness in different ecological niches

This overlay integrates capsid protein predictions with genome features to estimate overall virion robustness.

## Visualization Type
Composite dashboard with:
- Radial stability gauge (0-100 stability score)
- Protein hydrophobicity heatmap for major capsid protein
- Predicted disulfide bond network diagram
- Environmental tolerance ranges (pH, temperature)
- Comparison to known stable/fragile phages

## Worker Integration
Create `workers/stability-worker.ts`:
- Predict major capsid protein structure using sequence motifs
- Calculate hydrophobic moments and surface charge distribution
- Identify potential disulfide bonds (cysteine pairs)
- Estimate isoelectric point and thermal stability
- Integrate genome features (terminal repeats, DNA modifications)
- Compare against database of characterized phages

## Implementation Details

### Files to Create
1. `src/tui/overlays/VirionStabilityOverlay.tsx`
   - Dashboard layout with multiple panels
   - Radial gauge component for overall stability
   - Protein structure visualization
   - Environmental tolerance charts

2. `src/workers/stability-worker.ts`
   - Capsid protein prediction and analysis
   - Hydrophobicity calculations (Kyte-Doolittle scale)
   - Disulfide bond prediction algorithms
   - Stability scoring model
   - Database comparison logic

### Keyboard Shortcut
- **Alt+V** - Toggle virion stability overlay

### Data Flow
1. User activates overlay (Alt+V)
2. Worker receives genome sequence and ORF predictions
3. Worker identifies major capsid protein (MCP):
   - Search for conserved HK97-fold domain
   - Identify by size and gene position heuristics
4. Worker analyzes MCP:
   - Hydrophobicity profile
   - Cysteine positions for disulfide bonds
   - Charged residue distribution
   - Predicted pI and thermal stability
5. Worker generates composite stability score
6. UI renders dashboard with all panels

## Stability Scoring Model
Weighted composite of:
- **Capsid protein stability (40%)**: Hydrophobic core strength, disulfide bonds
- **Genome packaging (30%)**: Terminal repeats, modified bases, internal pressure
- **Environmental factors (30%)**: pI, predicted pH tolerance, thermal stability

## Display Panels
1. **Overall Stability Gauge**: 0-100 score with classification
2. **Protein Hydrophobicity Heatmap**: Residue-level visualization
3. **Disulfide Network**: Graph diagram of predicted bonds
4. **Environmental Tolerance**: Bar charts for pH, temperature ranges
5. **Similar Phages**: Top 3 matches with known stability data

## Testing Considerations
- Validate with experimentally characterized stable phages (T4, P22)
- Compare with fragile phages (temperate phages with reduced stability)
- Check correlation with known environmental persistence data","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:01:00.538706-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-08T17:21:28.263116-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.9","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:01:00.543963-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-bjoz.9","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-qiaq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-08T13:01:00.545002-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

-{"id":"phage_explorer-idhh","title":"Touch scroll momentum and inertia tuning","description":"# Task: Touch scroll momentum and inertia tuning

## Problem
The current touch scrolling works but feels "off" compared to native iOS/Android scrolling. Issues:
- Momentum decay is too linear
- Edge bounce effect missing
- Scroll snapping to positions would help for codon alignment

## Solution
Improve VirtualScroller touch handling to feel native:

### Momentum Physics
```typescript
// Natural feeling momentum using exponential decay
private applyMomentum() {
  const FRICTION = 0.95;  // Higher = longer coast
  const MIN_VELOCITY = 0.5;  // Stop threshold
  
  const decay = () => {
    this.velocity *= FRICTION;
    this.scrollPosition += this.velocity;
    
    // Clamp to bounds with rubber-band effect
    if (this.scrollPosition < 0) {
      this.scrollPosition *= 0.5;  // Rubber band
      this.velocity *= -0.3;  // Bounce back
    }
    
    if (Math.abs(this.velocity) > MIN_VELOCITY) {
      requestAnimationFrame(decay);
    } else {
      this.snapToNearestCodon();  // Optional snapping
    }
  };
  
  requestAnimationFrame(decay);
}
```

### Codon Snapping (Optional)
After scroll ends, optionally snap to nearest codon boundary:
```typescript
private snapToNearestCodon() {
  if (!this.snapEnabled) return;
  const codonSize = this.cellWidth * 3;
  const nearestCodon = Math.round(this.scrollPosition / codonSize) * codonSize;
  this.animateTo(nearestCodon, 150);  // 150ms snap animation
}
```

### Overscroll Effect
Add iOS-style overscroll at boundaries:
- Allow scrolling 50px past boundaries
- Spring back with elastic animation
- Subtle visual cue (glow or color shift)

## Parameters to Tune

| Parameter | Current | Proposed | Effect |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|
| friction | 0.92 | 0.95 | Longer coast |
| rubberBand | none | 0.5 | Edge elasticity |
| snapEnabled | false | optional | Codon alignment |
| minVelocity | 1.0 | 0.5 | Smoother stop |

## Files to Modify

- `/packages/web/src/rendering/VirtualScroller.ts` - Core momentum physics
- `/packages/web/src/hooks/useSequenceGrid.ts` - Expose snap preference
- `/packages/web/src/components/SequenceView.tsx` - Add snap toggle to settings

## Testing

1. Compare side-by-side with native iOS scroll
2. Test on various devices (touch sensitivity differs)
3. Performance test: ensure 60fps during momentum
4. Test with accessibility settings (reduced motion)

private applyMomentum() {

    this.velocity *= FRICTION;
